response:
THE LAW OF MOORE, FORMULATED BY GORDON MOORE IN 1965, PREDICTS THAT THE NUMBER OF TRANSISTORS ON A MICROCHIP DOUBLES ABOUT EVERY TWO YEARS. THIS OBSERVATION HAS DRIVEN THE RAPID ADVANCEMENT OF COMPUTING TECHNOLOGY FOR DECADES. HOWEVER, AS TECHNOLOGY HAS ADVANCED, THE PHYSICAL LIMITATIONS OF SILICON BASED INTEGRATED CIRCUITS HAVE BECOME MORE PREVALENT. MANUFACTURING PROCESS NODES HAVE REACHED SUB-10 NANOMETER SIZES, AND THE COSTS OF FABRICATING SMALLER TRANSISTORS HAVE INCREASED SIGNIFICANTLY. THESE FACTORS HAVE LED TO A SLOWING OF THE RATE OF INCREASE IN TRANSISTOR DENSITY, CAUSING MANY TO SPECULATE THAT THE LAW OF MOORE MAY EVENTUALLY CEASE TO HOLD TRUE.

***  
THE FUTURE OF THE LAW OF MOORE DEPENDS ON INNOVATIONS IN MATERIALS AND TECHNOLOGY, SUCH AS QUANTUM COMPUTING, THREE-DIMENSIONAL CHIP ARCHITECTURES, AND NEW TYPES OF SEMICONDUCTORS LIKE GALLIUM NITRIDE AND TIN SELENIDE. THESE ADVANCES MAY EXTEND THE LIFE OF THE LAW OF MOORE OR REDEFINE IT IN A DIFFERENT CONTEXT. HOWEVER, IT IS DIFFICULT TO PREDICT EXACTLY WHEN OR IF THE LAW WILL STOP APPLYING. MOST EXPERTS AGREE THAT WHILE THE LAW MAY EVOLVE, IT WILL NOT DISAPPEAR ENTIRELY. DO
